blocks.text: An Artificial Neural Network (ANN) is a computational model loosely inspired by the brain, composed of layers of interconnected nodes (neurons). Architecture: Input Layer → Hidden Layer(s) → Output Layer. Each neuron computes a weighted sum of inputs plus a bias, then applies a non-linear activation function: output = activation(Σwᵢxᵢ + b). The non-linearity is critical — without it, multiple linear layers collapse to a single linear transformation, eliminating any advantage. Universal approximation theorem: a neural network with one hidden layer of sufficient width can approximate any continuous function — theoretical justification for why ANNs work. In practice, depth (more layers) is more parameter-efficient than width. Deep networks learn hierarchical representations: early layers learn edges/phonemes, middle layers learn shapes/words, final layers learn concepts/sentences. Forward pass: compute predictions layer by layer. Backward pass (backpropagation): compute gradients and update weights.

A neural network is like a company with departments (layers). Input data enters the company (input layer), each department processes and transforms the information (hidden layers), and the final output is a decision or product (output layer). Each employee (neuron) specialises in detecting a particular pattern. The CEO (loss function) evaluates the final output and gives performance feedback (gradients) that propagates back to improve each department.

Key ANN facts: non-linear activation = essential. Depth > width for efficiency. Universal approximation = theoretical power. Training requires backprop + gradient descent. Overfitting controlled via dropout, regularisation, early stopping. ANN is the foundation — CNN adds spatial structure, RNN adds temporal structure, Transformers add global attention.

qas.q: Why do neural networks need non-linear activation functions?
qas.a: Without non-linearity, the composition of linear transformations W₃(W₂(W₁x)) = Wx is still a single linear transformation. No depth helps — you could collapse any deep network to one layer. Non-linear activations (ReLU, sigmoid, tanh) allow the network to learn complex, non-linear decision boundaries and hierarchical representations.

qas.q: What is the Universal Approximation Theorem?
qas.a: It states that a feedforward neural network with a single hidden layer and enough neurons can approximate any continuous function to arbitrary precision. This theorem guarantees expressiveness in theory. In practice, depth (multiple layers) is far more efficient than extreme width — deep networks learn compositional representations with exponentially fewer parameters.

blocks.text: Neural network fundamentals are tested before any deep learning interview. Know: ANN architecture, why non-linearity is essential, forward/backward pass distinction, and that depth > width. The activation function question is almost universal — be ready with ReLU, sigmoid, tanh, and when to use each.

Activation functions introduce non-linearity into neural networks. Key functions: Sigmoid: σ(x) = 1/(1+e⁻ˣ). Range (0,1). Problems: saturates at extreme values → vanishing gradients, outputs not zero-centred (slows convergence). Use: binary classification output layer. Tanh: tanh(x) = (eˣ−e⁻ˣ)/(eˣ+e⁻ˣ). Range (-1,1). Zero-centred (better than sigmoid) but still saturates. Use: hidden layers (historically). ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit): f(x) = max(0,x). Computationally cheap, no saturation for positive values, promotes sparse activation. Problem: Dying ReLU — neurons can get stuck outputting 0 if weights make all inputs negative (gradient = 0, weight never updates). Use: most hidden layers in modern networks. Leaky ReLU: f(x) = max(αx, x) (α≈0.01). Fixes dying ReLU. ELU, SELU: smooth variants with better properties. Softmax: converts vector to probability distribution — used for multi-class output layers. GELU (Gaussian Error Linear Unit): used in Transformers (GPT, BERT) — smooth approximation of ReLU.

Activation cheat sheet:
  Sigmoid   : output layer, binary classification; avoid hidden layers (vanishing grad)
  Tanh      : hidden layers (legacy); zero-centred, still saturates
  ReLU      : default choice for hidden layers; fast, effective
  Leaky ReLU: when dying ReLU is observed in training
  Softmax   : output layer, multi-class classification
  GELU      : Transformers, modern LLMs (GPT, BERT)
  Linear    : output layer for regression (unbounded output)

Vanishing gradient: sigmoid/tanh saturate at extremes (gradient ≈ 0) → gradients vanish as they multiply through many layers → early layers stop learning. ReLU solves this for positive values. Deep networks default to ReLU hidden layers because of this. Exploding gradient: opposite problem — gradients grow exponentially. Fixed by gradient clipping.

qas.q: What is the vanishing gradient problem?
qas.a: During backpropagation, gradients are multiplied layer by layer (chain rule). Sigmoid/tanh derivatives are always < 0.25. Multiplied 50 times: 0.25⁵⁰ ≈ 10⁻³⁰ — gradients become negligibly small in early layers, which stop learning. ReLU derivative is 1 for positive inputs (doesn't shrink) and 0 for negative (dying ReLU). ReLU largely solves vanishing gradients in feedforward networks.

qas.q: What is the dying ReLU problem?
qas.a: ReLU outputs 0 for all negative inputs. If a neuron receives only negative inputs (e.g., due to a large negative bias), its gradient is always 0 — the weight never updates. The neuron is 'dead' — it contributes nothing to the network forever. Solutions: Leaky ReLU (small slope for negative inputs), ELU, careful weight initialisation, lower learning rate.

blocks.text: Activation function questions are very common. The complete answer: why ReLU > sigmoid for hidden layers (vanishing gradient), dying ReLU and Leaky ReLU fix, Softmax for multi-class output, GELU for Transformers. Also know He initialisation with ReLU (initialise weights ∝ √(2/fan_in)).

Backpropagation computes the gradient of the loss with respect to every weight in the network using the chain rule. Forward pass: compute activations layer by layer and the final loss. Backward pass: starting from the loss, compute gradients layer by layer going backward. For each weight w: ∂L/∂w = ∂L/∂output × ∂output/∂w (chain rule). These gradients are used by gradient descent to update weights: w ← w − α × ∂L/∂w. An epoch is one complete pass through the entire training dataset. Batch: the subset of training data processed before a single weight update. Full Batch GD: uses all data per update — precise gradients but slow and requires full data in memory. Stochastic GD (SGD): one sample per update — very noisy gradients, fast iteration. Mini-Batch GD: uses a batch of 32-512 samples — balances noise and efficiency, standard in practice. The noisy gradient updates of SGD actually help escape local minima and provide a form of implicit regularisation.

Backprop = chain rule applied repeatedly backward through the computation graph. It's the same computation for any depth of network. Key insight: gradients flow backward in the exact reverse order of the forward pass. Vanishing/exploding gradients are the pathological cases of this. Batch size: larger = more stable gradients, less regularisation effect. Smaller = noisier, better generalisation, fits less in GPU memory.

qas.q: What is the difference between SGD, Mini-Batch GD, and Full-Batch GD?
qas.a: Full-Batch: gradients computed on entire dataset — precise but memory-intensive and slow per update. SGD (Stochastic): one sample per update — fast, memory-efficient, very noisy gradients, good implicit regularisation. Mini-Batch: k samples (32-512) per update — balances noise and efficiency, GPU-efficient (parallelism), standard in practice. In modern deep learning, 'SGD' almost always means mini-batch SGD.

qas.q: What is an epoch vs an iteration (step)?
qas.a: Epoch: one complete pass over the entire training dataset. Iteration/step: one forward+backward pass over a single mini-batch. If you have 10,000 samples and batch size 100, one epoch = 100 iterations. Training for 10 epochs = 1,000 weight update steps. Monitor validation loss per epoch (or every N steps for large datasets).

blocks.text: Backprop is foundational. Every interview touching neural networks may include 'explain backpropagation.' The non-technical answer: it efficiently computes gradients for all weights using the chain rule in one backward pass. The technical answer: ∂L/∂wᵢⱼ = ∂L/∂zⱼ × aᵢ where zⱼ is the pre-activation of neuron j and aᵢ is the activation feeding into it.

Dropout is a regularisation technique that randomly sets a fraction p (dropout rate) of neuron activations to zero during each training step. At test time, all neurons are used but weights are scaled by (1-p). Effect: prevents co-adaptation (neurons cannot rely on specific other neurons being present), forces the network to learn redundant representations. The ensemble interpretation: dropout trains ~2ⁿ different thinned networks (exponential in n neurons) implicitly averaged at test time. Batch Normalisation (BatchNorm) normalises the inputs to each layer across the batch: x̂ = (x − μ_batch) / (σ_batch + ε), then applies learned scale γ and shift β. Effects: stabilises training (reduces internal covariate shift), allows higher learning rates, reduces dependence on initialisation, mild regularisation effect. Typically placed after the linear transform and before the activation function. Both are essential tools for training deep networks.

Dropout: train with random node removal, test with all nodes scaled. Prevents co-adaptation. Rate 0.2-0.5 for hidden layers. Rate 0.0-0.2 for output layers. BatchNorm: normalise each layer's input per batch, then let the network re-scale. Speeds up training, allows larger learning rate. Both are regularisation in different senses.

qas.q: Why does dropout work as regularisation?
qas.a: Dropout prevents any neuron from becoming overly specialised (co-adaptation). A neuron cannot learn to detect a pattern only in conjunction with specific other neurons because those others may be dropped. The network must distribute the responsibility for detecting each feature across many neurons — a form of ensemble learning. This creates more robust, generalisable representations.

qas.q: What problem does Batch Normalisation solve?
qas.a: Internal covariate shift: the distribution of inputs to each layer changes as the previous layer's weights update. This slows training because each layer must continuously adapt to a shifting input distribution. BatchNorm normalises inputs to each layer, making the distribution stable. This allows higher learning rates, faster convergence, and reduces the need for careful weight initialisation.

blocks.text: BatchNorm and Dropout are expected knowledge for any deep learning role. Know: Dropout trains subnetworks, scales at test time. BatchNorm normalises per batch with learnable γ/β. BatchNorm is placed before activation. In Transformers, LayerNorm is used instead of BatchNorm (normalises across the feature dimension, not the batch).

CNNs apply learned filters (kernels) to local regions of the input to detect features. A convolution operation slides a filter over the input, computing a dot product at each position, producing a feature map. Key components: Convolution Layer — applies F filters of size K×K, learning spatial features. Activation (ReLU) — non-linearity after convolution. Pooling (Max/Avg) — reduces spatial dimensions, provides translation invariance. Flatten — converts feature maps to a 1D vector. Fully Connected Layer — final classification/regression. Key concepts: Receptive field — the region of input that influences a neuron. Stride — how many pixels to move the filter per step (stride 2 = halves spatial dimensions). Padding — zero-pad input borders to preserve spatial size (same padding) or shrink it (valid padding). Parameter sharing — one filter is applied across the entire input (unlike a dense layer) — dramatically fewer parameters. Translation invariance — due to pooling, CNNs recognise features regardless of where they appear in the image. CNNs dominate image tasks; they also work for text/audio with 1D convolutions.

CNN architecture example (for image 224×224×3):
  Input:         224×224×3
  Conv2D(64, 3×3, stride=1, same) → 224×224×64
  MaxPool(2×2, stride=2)          → 112×112×64
  Conv2D(128, 3×3)                → 112×112×128
  MaxPool(2×2)                    → 56×56×128
  Flatten                         → 401,408
  Dense(1024, ReLU)               → 1024
  Dense(10, Softmax)              → 10 class probabilities

CNN key advantages over fully-connected for images: parameter sharing (one filter scans whole image — millions fewer params), translation invariance (pooling = 'where' matters less than 'what'), hierarchical features (edge → texture → shape → object). ResNet solves deep CNN training: skip connections allow gradients to flow directly, enabling 100+ layer networks.

qas.q: Why are CNNs more efficient than fully-connected networks for images?
qas.a: A 256×256×3 image has 196,608 inputs. A fully-connected layer with 1000 neurons needs 196M parameters. A 3×3 convolution filter needs only 27 parameters and is shared across all positions — capturing the same feature everywhere with the same weights. CNNs exploit the spatial structure of images: nearby pixels are related, features appear at multiple locations. Fully-connected layers treat every pixel as independent.

qas.q: What is a residual connection (skip connection) in ResNet?
qas.a: A skip connection adds the input of a block directly to its output: output = F(x) + x. This allows gradients to flow directly through the skip path during backpropagation, bypassing multiple layers. Without skip connections, very deep networks (>20 layers) degrade in performance due to vanishing gradients. ResNet's key insight: learning residual F(x) = desired_output - input is easier than learning the full transformation directly.

blocks.text: CNN questions appear in vision ML interviews. Know: convolution mechanics (filter, stride, padding), pooling, parameter sharing rationale, and ResNet's skip connections. For 2025 interviews, mention Vision Transformers (ViT) have partially replaced CNNs for large-scale vision by applying self-attention to image patches — shows awareness of current trends.

chapters.title: RNN, Vanishing Gradient, LSTM, and GRU

blocks.text: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) process sequential data by maintaining a hidden state hₜ that carries information across timesteps: hₜ = tanh(Wₕhₜ₋₁ + Wₓxₜ + b). The same weights are shared across all timesteps (parameter sharing over time). Problem: Vanishing Gradient in RNNs — gradients are multiplied by W_h at each timestep during BPTT (Backpropagation Through Time). If W_h's eigenvalues are < 1, gradients vanish over long sequences; if > 1, they explode. This makes standard RNNs unable to learn long-range dependencies. LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) solves this with a cell state (long-term memory) and gating mechanisms: Forget Gate — what to erase from cell state. Input Gate — what new information to write. Output Gate — what to read from cell state. The cell state has an additive update path (not multiplicative) allowing gradients to flow without vanishing. GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit) simplifies LSTM with two gates (update + reset) and no separate cell state — fewer parameters, similar performance, faster to train.

Standard RNN is like passing a note down a chain of 1000 people — each person reads it and writes a new version. By person 1000, the original message is mangled beyond recognition. LSTM is like passing a letter in an envelope down the chain: each person can open the envelope (read), add a sticky note (write), or seal it back (forget), but the original letter stays protected inside the envelope. Long-range information survives intact.

LSTM cell state = conveyor belt carrying long-term information. Gates control what gets added/removed/read. Vanishing gradient in LSTM is solved by additive (not multiplicative) cell state updates. GRU = simpler LSTM (2 gates instead of 3, no cell state). For modern NLP, Transformers have replaced RNNs/LSTMs — they capture long-range dependencies more effectively and parallelise during training.

qas.q: Why do standard RNNs fail for long sequences?
qas.a: During BPTT, gradients at timestep t are computed by multiplying the gradient by the weight matrix W_h repeatedly. For sequences of length 100, the gradient contains W_h^100. If eigenvalues of W_h < 1, the gradient vanishes (approaches 0). If > 1, it explodes. Long-range dependencies require gradient signals to propagate hundreds of steps — impossible with multiplicative decay.

qas.q: How does LSTM solve the vanishing gradient problem?
qas.a: The LSTM cell state update is additive: c_t = f_t × c_{t-1} + i_t × ĉ_t. The forget gate f_t can be set near 1, making the gradient path ∂c_t/∂c_{t-1} ≈ 1 (highway for gradients). The cell state provides a direct gradient path over many timesteps without multiplicative decay. The gates are learned to selectively preserve information that is relevant for long-range dependencies.

blocks.text: LSTM is asked when discussing sequential data, NLP, or time-series. Know: vanishing gradient cause (multiplicative gradients through W_h), LSTM's three gates and cell state solution, GRU as a simpler alternative, and that Transformers have largely superseded both for NLP. For tabular time-series, LSTM still competes with XGBoost.

Transfer learning uses a model trained on a large dataset (source task) as a starting point for a different but related task (target task), rather than training from scratch. Motivation: training large models from scratch requires massive data and compute. Pretrained models already know useful representations (edges/textures for vision, word/sentence semantics for NLP). Transfer learning approaches: Feature Extraction — freeze pretrained model weights, add new classification head, train only the head. Fast, needs little data. Fine-tuning — unfreeze some or all pretrained layers, train end-to-end on target task with a small learning rate. Better performance, needs more data. Zero-shot transfer — use a model directly on a new task with no fine-tuning (GPT-4 reasoning about new domains, CLIP matching images to text). In NLP: pretrained BERT/GPT → fine-tune on your dataset. In vision: pretrained ResNet/ViT → fine-tune on your dataset. Rule: the more similar the source and target tasks, the more layers you can freeze.

Transfer learning is the reason a 100-line fine-tuning script can achieve state-of-the-art on a medical imaging task with 1000 samples. The pretrained model brings millions of examples worth of representation learning. Small learning rate for fine-tuning (0.00001-0.0001) to avoid destroying pretrained representations.

qas.q: When would you use feature extraction vs fine-tuning in transfer learning?
qas.a: Feature extraction (freeze all, train head only): when your target dataset is small (<1000 samples) and similar to the source task. Fine-tuning risks overwriting useful pretrained weights. Full fine-tuning (train all layers): when you have enough data (>10k samples) and the target task differs from the source. Intermediate: freeze early layers (general features), fine-tune later layers (task-specific features).

qas.q: What is catastrophic forgetting in transfer learning?
qas.a: When fine-tuning a pretrained model on a new task, aggressive training can overwrite the general representations learned during pretraining — the model forgets its previous knowledge. Mitigation: use a very small learning rate (0.00001), use learning rate warm-up, freeze early layers (they contain general representations), use regularisation techniques like Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC).

blocks.text: Transfer learning is asked in nearly every deep learning interview. 'How would you build an image classifier with only 500 labelled images?' Answer: fine-tune a pretrained ResNet/EfficientNet. The key nuance: freeze early layers (low-level features are universal), fine-tune final layers (task-specific) with a small learning rate. Mention LoRA for LLM fine-tuning if the context is NLP.

Text preprocessing transforms raw text into a format suitable for ML models. Key steps: Tokenisation — splitting text into individual tokens (words, subwords, or characters). Word tokenisation splits by whitespace/punctuation. Subword tokenisation (BPE, WordPiece) handles unknown words by splitting them into subwords — used by BERT, GPT. Lowercasing — normalises case (Apple, apple → apple). Stopword Removal — removes common words with little meaning (the, a, is). Helps classical ML; harmful for modern transformers (context matters). Stemming — heuristically reduces words to root form (running → run, better → bett). Fast but produces non-words. Lemmatisation — reduces words to their dictionary base form using linguistic knowledge (running → run, better → good). Slower but linguistically correct. POS Tagging — tags each word with its part of speech (noun, verb, adjective). Used for NER and parsing. Named Entity Recognition (NER) — identifies named entities (person, organisation, location) in text. Modern NLP (Transformers) requires minimal preprocessing — tokenisation only. Classical NLP (TF-IDF, bag-of-words) benefits from the full pipeline.

Modern NLP (BERT, GPT): tokenise only (BPE/WordPiece handles everything). Classical NLP (TF-IDF, Naive Bayes): lowercase → tokenise → remove stopwords → stem/lemmatise → feature vector. Stemming is faster but messier. Lemmatisation is cleaner but requires a dictionary. Both reduce vocabulary size and normalise morphological variation.

qas.q: What is the difference between stemming and lemmatisation?
qas.a: Stemming heuristically chops word endings using rule-based algorithms (Porter stemmer): 'running' → 'run', 'better' → 'bett'. Fast but produces non-words. Lemmatisation uses a vocabulary and morphological analysis to return the base dictionary form (lemma): 'running' → 'run', 'better' → 'good'. Accurate but slower. For classical NLP, lemmatisation is preferred for quality; stemming for speed.

qas.q: What is subword tokenisation and why is it used?
qas.a: Subword tokenisation (BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece) splits words into common subword units. 'unhappy' → ['un', 'happy']. Advantages: handles out-of-vocabulary words (new words not in training vocab can still be tokenised as subwords). Fixed vocabulary size. Balances word-level (too many tokens for rare words) and character-level (sequences too long). Used by BERT (WordPiece), GPT (BPE).

blocks.text: Text preprocessing questions set the stage for NLP discussions. Key: for modern transformer models, preprocessing is minimal (tokenise, no stemming needed). For classical NLP systems, the full pipeline applies. Mentioning BPE/WordPiece subword tokenisation shows awareness of modern NLP practice.

Word embeddings are dense vector representations of words that capture semantic meaning. Unlike one-hot encoding (sparse, no semantic information), embeddings encode relationships: 'king' − 'man' + 'woman' ≈ 'queen'. Word2Vec: trains a shallow neural network on one of two tasks — CBOW (predict target word from context) or Skip-gram (predict context words from target word). The hidden layer weights become the word embeddings. Training objective encourages semantically similar words to have similar vectors. GloVe (Global Vectors): uses global word co-occurrence statistics from a corpus rather than local context windows. Factorises the co-occurrence matrix. Often comparable to Word2Vec but captures global corpus statistics better. FastText: extends Word2Vec by representing each word as a bag of character n-grams — handles morphologically rich languages and out-of-vocabulary words. Limitation: static embeddings — 'bank' has the same vector in 'bank robbery' and 'river bank.' Contextual embeddings (BERT, GPT) solve this.

Word embeddings are like assigning coordinates to words in a semantic city. Words with similar meanings cluster in the same neighbourhood. 'Doctor' and 'physician' are next-door neighbours. 'King' and 'queen' are in the same district but different streets — separated by a 'gender' direction in the coordinate space. The vector arithmetic king − man + woman ≈ queen means moving in the 'gender' direction preserves regal meaning.

qas.q: What is the Skip-gram model in Word2Vec?
qas.a: Skip-gram takes a target word and tries to predict the surrounding context words within a window. Given 'The cat sat on the mat' with window=2 and target='sat', the model tries to predict ['The', 'cat', 'on', 'the']. The neural network learns weights that encode the target word such that nearby words are predicted well. These weights become the word vector. Skip-gram works better for rare words; CBOW is faster for frequent words.

qas.q: What is the main limitation of static word embeddings like Word2Vec?
qas.a: Static embeddings assign one vector per word regardless of context. 'Bank' has the same vector whether the sentence is 'I robbed the bank' or 'The river bank flooded.' Polysemy (multiple meanings) cannot be captured. Contextual embeddings (BERT, GPT) assign different vectors to the same word depending on its context — this was the key advance that enabled modern NLP.

blocks.text: Word embeddings bridge classical and modern NLP. Know: Word2Vec (CBOW vs Skip-gram), that similar words have similar vectors, static embedding limitation (one vector per word), and that BERT/GPT produce contextual embeddings. The analogy question ('king - man + woman = queen') is a famous example always worth knowing.

Attention mechanisms allow neural networks to focus on relevant parts of the input when generating each output, rather than compressing everything into a fixed-size vector. Originally proposed for seq2seq translation: when translating 'chat' (French) to 'cat' (English), the decoder attends to the relevant encoder hidden states. Scaled Dot-Product Attention: given Query Q, Key K, Value V matrices: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QKᵀ/√d_k)V. Intuition: queries are what you want to find, keys are what's available, values are the information attached to each key. The dot product QKᵀ measures similarity between query and each key; softmax normalises to attention weights (sum to 1); multiply by V to get a weighted combination of values. Self-attention: Q, K, V all come from the same sequence — each position attends to all other positions in the same sequence. This allows each word to gather context from every other word in one operation (unlike RNNs which process left-to-right). Multi-head attention: run h parallel attention heads with different learned projections, concatenate outputs — each head focuses on different types of relationships (syntax, semantics, coreference).

Attention is like a librarian who, when asked 'find me information about neural networks', scans all book titles (keys) for relevance to the query, picks the most relevant books (softmax weights), and brings a weighted selection of their contents (values) back to you. The output is a personalised, query-dependent blend of all available information — not just the nearest book.

Q, K, V: Query = what you want, Key = what exists, Value = the information content. Softmax(QKᵀ/√d_k) = attention weights (who to pay attention to). Scale by 1/√d_k prevents dot products from getting too large before softmax (which would make gradients vanish). Self-attention = O(n²) time and memory (every position attends to every other) — the quadratic bottleneck for long sequences.

qas.q: Why is attention O(n²) and why does this matter?
qas.a: Self-attention computes pairwise similarities between all n positions: Q×Kᵀ is an n×n matrix. Time and memory are O(n²×d). For short sequences (n=512), manageable. For long contexts (n=100k), prohibitively expensive. Modern solutions: FlashAttention (reorders computation for memory efficiency), Sparse Attention (attend to only local or sampled positions), Sliding Window Attention (Longformer), Linear Attention approximations.

qas.q: What is multi-head attention?
qas.a: Multi-head attention runs h parallel attention functions with different linear projections of Q, K, V. Each head learns to attend to different types of relationships (one head may focus on syntactic dependencies, another on semantic similarity, another on coreference). The outputs are concatenated and projected: MultiHead(Q,K,V) = Concat(head₁,...,headₕ)Wᴼ. h=8 or 12 heads is common.

blocks.text: Attention is the core mechanism of Transformers. Every LLM/NLP interview asks about it. Know: Q/K/V intuition, scaled dot-product formula, self-attention, multi-head attention purpose, and the O(n²) limitation. Mentioning FlashAttention shows 2024-2025 depth.

The Transformer architecture (Vaswani et al., 'Attention Is All You Need', 2017) replaced recurrence with self-attention, enabling full parallelisation during training and better long-range dependency modelling. Architecture: Encoder stack (BERT) — processes input bidirectionally; each encoder layer has multi-head self-attention + feedforward network + residual connections + layer norm. Decoder stack (GPT) — generates output autoregressively; each decoder layer has masked self-attention (can only attend to past tokens) + cross-attention (attends to encoder output) + feedforward. Positional encoding: attention has no notion of order (unlike RNNs). Positional encodings add information about token positions using sinusoidal functions or learned embeddings. Encoder-only (BERT): good for understanding tasks (classification, NER, QA). Decoder-only (GPT): good for generation tasks (text completion, summarisation). Encoder-Decoder (T5, BART): good for seq2seq tasks (translation, summarisation).

Transformer encoder layer (one of N identical layers):
  Input → Multi-Head Self-Attention → Add & Norm → Feed-Forward → Add & Norm → Output
  Residual: Add & Norm = LayerNorm(x + Sublayer(x))
  Feed-Forward: two linear layers with ReLU: FFN(x) = max(0, xW₁ + b₁)W₂ + b₂
  Typical dimensions: d_model=512, 8 heads, d_ff=2048, N=6 layers

Transformers: parallelise (vs RNN sequential), bidirectional (BERT) or causal (GPT), O(n²) attention. Key innovations: multi-head attention (parallel attention patterns), residual connections (deep network stability), layer norm (stable training), positional encoding (injects sequence order). These four innovations together made the architecture trainable at scale.

qas.q: What is the key advantage of Transformers over RNNs?
qas.a: Parallelisation: RNNs process tokens sequentially — token t can only be computed after token t-1. Training on sequences of length 1000 = 1000 sequential steps. Transformers compute all positions simultaneously via self-attention. This enables training on much longer sequences and using massive parallelism (GPU utilisation). Additionally, Transformers model long-range dependencies in a single attention step regardless of distance, while RNNs must propagate information step by step.

qas.q: Why do Transformers need positional encodings?
qas.a: Self-attention is permutation-invariant — shuffling the input tokens produces the same attention weights. The model has no built-in sense of word order. Positional encodings add position information by injecting a position-dependent vector into each token embedding. Sinusoidal encodings (original): PE(pos,2i) = sin(pos/10000^(2i/d)) — allows extrapolation to unseen lengths. Learned positional embeddings (GPT) — more flexible but fixed maximum length.

blocks.text: Transformers are the foundation of modern AI. Every serious NLP/LLM interview will include transformer architecture questions. Know: encoder vs decoder vs encoder-decoder and when to use each, the four key components (self-attention, FFN, residual, layer norm), and why it replaced RNNs (parallelisation + long-range dependencies). The follow-up will be BERT or GPT details.

BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers, Google 2018): encoder-only Transformer pretrained with two self-supervised tasks — Masked Language Modelling (MLM: predict randomly masked tokens, using both left and right context) and Next Sentence Prediction (NSP: predict if two sentences are consecutive). Bidirectionality means each token attends to all other tokens — excellent for understanding tasks. Fine-tune by adding a task-specific head. BERT base: 12 layers, 768 hidden size, 12 attention heads, 110M parameters. GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer, OpenAI): decoder-only Transformer pretrained with causal language modelling (CLM: predict the next token using only left context). Causal masking means each token can only attend to past tokens. Optimised for generation. GPT-4 and beyond: same architecture, vastly more parameters (estimated 1T), more data, RLHF alignment. Key difference: BERT = bidirectional = understanding. GPT = autoregressive = generation. For classification tasks, BERT-style models are preferred. For open-ended generation, GPT-style models.

BERT: Bidirectional, Encoder, MLM pretraining. Best for: classification, NER, question answering, embeddings. GPT: Generative (left-to-right), Decoder, CLM pretraining. Best for: text generation, chat, completion, code. T5 (Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer): encoder-decoder, frames every NLP task as text-in text-out. RoBERTa = BERT trained longer on more data (better baseline than original BERT).

qas.q: What is Masked Language Modelling (MLM) in BERT?
qas.a: During pretraining, 15% of tokens are randomly masked. The model must predict the original token from the surrounding context (using both left and right context — bidirectional). This forces the model to learn deep contextual representations. Unlike GPT's left-to-right prediction, MLM uses the full bidirectional context at every position, making BERT representations richer for understanding tasks.

qas.q: Why is GPT better for generation than BERT?
qas.a: GPT uses causal (left-to-right) masking — each token is predicted from only past tokens, exactly matching the generation process (you generate token by token left to right). BERT uses both left and right context — you cannot autoregressively generate because generating token t requires knowing tokens after t, which don't exist yet during generation.

blocks.text: BERT vs GPT is a cornerstone NLP question. Know: BERT = encoder = bidirectional = understanding; GPT = decoder = autoregressive = generation. Why BERT can't generate, why GPT can't do MLM. For 2025 interviews, also mention that instruction-tuned GPT models (ChatGPT, GPT-4) with RLHF dramatically improved following human instructions.

chapters.title: What is a Large Language Model (LLM)?

blocks.text: A Large Language Model (LLM) is a deep neural network trained on vast text corpora to model the probability distribution of text: P(token_n | token_1, ..., token_{n-1}). Given a sequence, it predicts the most likely next token. Scaled up massively, this simple objective produces models capable of reasoning, coding, summarisation, translation, and question answering. LLMs are decoder-only Transformers (GPT-style) with billions to trillions of parameters. Training stages: (1) Pretraining — train on internet-scale text (books, web, code) with next-token prediction. (2) Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) — fine-tune on high-quality instruction-response pairs. (3) RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) — align with human preferences using reward model + PPO. Scale laws (Chinchilla): optimal training = scale model parameters and data proportionally. Key capability: in-context learning — the ability to follow new tasks described in the prompt without weight updates (zero-shot, few-shot learning).

An LLM is an incredibly sophisticated next-word autocomplete, trained on all human text ever written. When you type 'The capital of France is', it completes 'Paris' not because it 'knows' geography, but because 'Paris' is the overwhelmingly most likely continuation of that text sequence in training data. Scaled to 1 trillion parameters trained on trillions of tokens, this simple autocomplete learns to reason, code, and converse.

LLM = next-token prediction at massive scale. Key stages: Pretrain (raw data) → SFT (instruction following) → RLHF (human preference alignment). The emergent capabilities (reasoning, coding, in-context learning) appear around 100B+ parameters — this is why smaller models are much worse at complex tasks.

qas.q: What is RLHF and why is it needed?
qas.a: RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) aligns LLM outputs with human preferences. After SFT, a reward model is trained on human comparisons (rater chooses which of two responses is better). The LLM is then fine-tuned with PPO (Proximal Policy Optimisation) to maximise the reward model's score. Without RLHF, LLMs produce technically fluent but unhelpful, biased, or harmful outputs. RLHF transforms a text completion model into a helpful assistant.

qas.q: What are emergent capabilities in LLMs?
qas.a: Capabilities that appear suddenly as scale increases and were not present in smaller models — not predictable from smaller-scale extrapolation. Examples: chain-of-thought reasoning (GPT-3.5+), arithmetic, code generation, few-shot learning. The emergence threshold differs per capability: some emerge at 10B parameters, others at 100B. Emergent capabilities are why LLMs exhibit qualitative jumps in ability with scale.

blocks.text: LLM fundamentals are mandatory knowledge for any 2025 engineering role. Know: training stages (pretrain → SFT → RLHF), that it's next-token prediction at scale, in-context learning without weight updates, and emergent capabilities from scale. The RLHF question distinguishes surface-level from deep knowledge.

Tokens are the basic unit of LLM input/output. A token is roughly 4 characters or 0.75 words in English. 'ChatGPT is amazing' ≈ 5 tokens. LLMs use subword tokenisation (BPE/WordPiece) — common words are single tokens, rare words split into subwords. Token count determines cost and speed (APIs charge per token). Context window is the maximum number of tokens an LLM can process at once (input + output). GPT-4: 128k tokens. Claude 3.5: 200k tokens. Gemini 1.5 Pro: 1M tokens. Everything within the context window is the model's 'working memory' — it can attend to any part of it. Beyond the context window, information is simply unavailable to the model. Temperature controls the randomness of token sampling. Temperature = 0: always pick the highest-probability next token (deterministic, repetitive). Temperature = 1: sample proportionally to probability distribution (creative, varied). Temperature > 1: more uniform distribution (very random, often incoherent). Top-p (nucleus sampling): sample from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability ≥ p — more principled than temperature alone.

Rule of thumb: 1000 tokens ≈ 750 words ≈ 1.5 pages. Context window = the LLM's RAM — what it can 'see' simultaneously. Temperature: low for factual answers, high for creative writing. Long context is expensive because attention is O(n²) — doubling context length = 4× the attention compute.

qas.q: What happens when input exceeds the context window?
qas.a: The LLM simply cannot process what's outside the window — it has no access to it. Earlier content must be truncated or summarised. For RAG systems, this is why retrieval is critical: you can't feed an entire knowledge base into the context (cost, latency, 'lost in the middle' problem). Effective strategies: chunk and retrieve relevant sections, summarise long documents first.

qas.q: What is the 'lost in the middle' problem?
qas.a: Research shows that LLMs perform worse at recalling information placed in the middle of a long context compared to information at the beginning or end. Even with 200k context windows, performance degrades for documents positioned in the middle. Practical implication: place the most important context near the beginning or end of the prompt. RAG (which retrieves only relevant chunks) partially mitigates this.

blocks.text: Token/context knowledge shows practical LLM engineering experience. Know: ≈4 chars/token, context window as working memory, temperature 0 for deterministic/factual output, temperature 0.7-1.0 for creative output. The 'lost in the middle' problem is a 2023-2024 research finding that shows reading depth of knowledge.

Embeddings are dense vector representations that encode semantic meaning. A text embedding maps a sentence to a vector of ~1536 dimensions (OpenAI ada-002) such that semantically similar sentences have high cosine similarity. Embedding models: text-embedding-ada-002 (OpenAI), sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (open source), Cohere Embed. Vector Databases store and index millions of high-dimensional vectors, enabling fast approximate nearest neighbour (ANN) search. Traditional databases index scalar values; vector DBs index vectors. Popular options: Pinecone (managed, easy), Weaviate (open source, hybrid search), Chroma (lightweight, local), Qdrant (Rust-based, fast), pgvector (PostgreSQL extension). ANN algorithms: HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World — graph-based, fast), IVF (Inverted File — quantisation-based, memory efficient). Use case: given a user query, embed it, search the vector DB for the K most similar document chunks, feed those chunks to the LLM as context (RAG pattern).

Embeddings = semantic fingerprints. Cosine similarity measures angle between vectors (1 = identical, 0 = unrelated, -1 = opposite). Vector DBs enable semantic search: 'find content similar in meaning to this query' — impossible with SQL LIKE or BM25 keyword search. The pipeline: document → chunk → embed → store in vector DB → query → embed query → ANN search → retrieve → pass to LLM.

qas.q: What is the difference between semantic search and keyword search?
qas.a: Keyword search (BM25, Elasticsearch): matches based on exact word overlap. Misses synonyms and paraphrases. Fast and interpretable. Semantic search (vector similarity): embeds query and documents, finds nearest vectors by cosine similarity. Catches synonyms and paraphrases — 'automobile' matches 'car'. Slower (ANN search) but understands meaning. Hybrid search combines both: use BM25 for recall and rerank with embeddings for precision.

qas.q: What is HNSW and why is it used for vector search?
qas.a: HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) is a graph-based ANN index. Builds a multi-layer graph where each node connects to nearby nodes (like a skip list for dense vectors). Search starts at the top layer (few connections, fast traversal) and drills down to finer layers. Provides sub-millisecond approximate nearest neighbour search for millions of vectors. Trade-off: high recall, fast query time, high memory usage (~4 bytes × dims × n vectors).

blocks.text: Embeddings + vector DBs are the infrastructure behind RAG systems. Know: cosine similarity for semantic matching, HNSW for fast ANN, and which vector DB to choose for different constraints. System design interviews on AI products (semantic search, chatbots, recommendation) will ask you to design this stack.

RAG combines retrieval (fetching relevant external knowledge) with generation (LLM response) to enable LLMs to answer questions about documents not in their training data without retraining. Architecture: (1) Indexing: split documents into chunks (300-500 tokens), embed each chunk, store in vector DB. (2) Retrieval: embed user query, search vector DB for top-K similar chunks (k=3-10). (3) Augmentation: add retrieved chunks to the LLM prompt as context. (4) Generation: LLM generates answer grounded in retrieved context. Problems RAG solves: outdated training knowledge (retrieval from live data), hallucinations (LLM can cite sources), knowledge too large for context window, domain-specific knowledge not in pretraining. RAG variants: Naive RAG (basic retrieval + generation). Advanced RAG: query rewriting, hypothetical document embeddings, hierarchical indexing, re-ranking (rerank retrieved chunks with a cross-encoder). Agentic RAG: multiple retrieval steps, tool calling.

Basic RAG pipeline:
  User Query
      ↓
  Embed query → Vector DB ANN search → top-K chunks
      ↓
  Prompt = 'Answer the question using only the context below.\n\nContext: {chunks}\n\nQuestion: {query}'
      ↓
  LLM generates grounded answer

RAG = knowledge retrieval + LLM reasoning. The LLM brings reasoning; the vector DB brings up-to-date knowledge. Key failure modes: (1) Retrieval fails — wrong chunks fetched (fix: hybrid search, query rewriting). (2) LLM ignores context — hallucination despite good retrieval (fix: stronger system prompt, smaller context, citation enforcement). (3) Chunk boundaries cut off context (fix: overlap between chunks, larger chunks).

qas.q: Why use RAG instead of fine-tuning to incorporate new knowledge?
qas.a: Fine-tuning: expensive (compute + data), knowledge is baked into weights (hard to update), risk of catastrophic forgetting, cannot cite sources. RAG: cheap (no retraining), knowledge is always up-to-date (update the vector DB), can cite specific source chunks, modular (change retrieval without touching the LLM). Rule of thumb: RAG for dynamic/external knowledge, fine-tuning for style/tone/capability adaptation.

qas.q: What are the main failure modes of a RAG system?
qas.a: Retrieval failure: semantically correct chunks not retrieved because embedding model doesn't understand domain (fix: fine-tune embeddings, use hybrid BM25+semantic search). Context stuffing: too many chunks confuse the LLM or hit context limits (fix: rerank, reduce K). Hallucination despite context: LLM ignores retrieved context and fabricates (fix: explicit instructions, citation enforcement, temperature=0). Chunking mismatch: information split across chunk boundaries (fix: overlap, sentence-aware chunking).

blocks.text: RAG is the most important AI engineering pattern of 2024-2025. Any system design involving an LLM with proprietary or up-to-date data requires RAG. Know the indexing pipeline, retrieval step, augmentation format, and failure modes. The RAG vs fine-tuning comparison is a standard interview question.

Prompt engineering is the practice of designing inputs to LLMs to reliably elicit desired outputs. Key techniques: Zero-shot: provide only the task description with no examples — relies entirely on the model's pretrained knowledge. Few-shot: provide 2-5 labelled examples of the task before the actual query — dramatically improves performance on specific formats or domains. Chain-of-Thought (CoT): ask the model to 'think step by step' before giving the answer. Forces intermediate reasoning, dramatically improves performance on math and logical reasoning. Self-consistency: sample multiple CoT reasoning paths, take the majority answer — reduces variance. System prompt: sets the model's persona, tone, constraints, and task definition (separate from the user message). Structured output: instruct the LLM to respond in JSON — ensures parseable outputs for downstream code. ReAct (Reason + Act): interleaves reasoning steps with tool calls — the foundation of AI agents. Role prompting: 'You are an expert Python developer' — primes the model's knowledge domain.

Prompt engineering hierarchy (increasing effectiveness): Zero-shot < Few-shot < CoT < CoT + few-shot + self-consistency. For structured output: always include JSON schema example in the prompt. For complex tasks: break into sub-tasks (decomposition). Anthropic's golden rules: be specific, give examples, define output format, set explicit constraints (length, style, what to avoid).

qas.q: Why does Chain-of-Thought prompting improve LLM performance?
qas.a: CoT forces the model to externalise its reasoning step-by-step. Complex problems (multi-step math, logical deduction) require intermediate computations — a single-step prediction compresses them away, losing accuracy. By generating reasoning tokens explicitly, the model can build on each step and correct itself. CoT emerged as a capability at ~100B parameter scale — smaller models do not benefit significantly.

qas.q: What is the difference between zero-shot and few-shot prompting?
qas.a: Zero-shot: no examples provided — model relies on pretraining to understand the task from description alone. Works for well-defined common tasks. Few-shot: 2-5 input-output examples are included in the prompt. Demonstrates the desired format, style, and task specification — more reliable for uncommon formats, domain-specific tasks, or strict output formatting requirements. Cost: each example adds tokens, increasing cost and latency.

blocks.text: Prompt engineering shows practical LLM experience. Know: zero-shot vs few-shot, CoT for reasoning, structured output prompts, system prompts. The sophisticated follow-up: 'How would you test a prompt?' Answer: create an evaluation dataset of representative inputs with expected outputs, run the prompt, measure with a metric (accuracy, exact match, LLM-as-judge). This shows production engineering mindset.

Fine-tuning adapts a pretrained model to a specific task or domain by continuing training on a labelled dataset. Full fine-tuning: updates all model parameters — best performance but requires storing and updating the full model (e.g., 70B float32 params ≈ 280GB memory). Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods update only a small fraction of parameters. LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation): instead of updating the full weight matrix W (d×d), add a low-rank decomposition ΔW = AB where A is d×r and B is r×d with r << d (typically r=8-64). Only A and B are trained (<<0.1% of parameters). At inference, merge: W' = W + AB. Advantages: 1000× fewer trainable parameters, original model weights unchanged (can serve multiple LoRAs on one base), fits on a single GPU for 7B-70B models. QLoRA: combines LoRA with 4-bit quantisation of the base model — fine-tune 70B models on a single 48GB GPU. Instruction fine-tuning: fine-tune on (instruction, response) pairs to improve instruction following.

LoRA key insight: weight updates during fine-tuning have low intrinsic rank — the important update can be captured in a much lower-dimensional subspace. Rank r controls the capacity of the adaptation (higher r = more expressive, more params). Alpha (lora_alpha) scales the LoRA contribution: scale = alpha/r. QLoRA = LoRA + 4-bit base = fine-tune 70B on one consumer GPU.

qas.q: How does LoRA work and why is it efficient?
qas.a: LoRA adds two small matrices A (d×r) and B (r×d) alongside each target weight W. During forward pass: h = Wx + (BA)x/scale. During training: only A and B are updated, W is frozen. Because r << d (e.g., r=16 vs d=4096), AB has 16×4096×2 = 131K parameters vs W's 4096×4096 = 16M — 100× fewer. Multiple LoRA adapters for different tasks can be swapped without reloading the base model.

qas.q: When would you use fine-tuning vs RAG vs prompt engineering?
qas.a: Prompt engineering: fastest, no data needed, for general tasks. RAG: for dynamic/external knowledge, factual grounding, no retraining needed. Fine-tuning: for style/tone adaptation, domain-specific language, consistent output format, or when a task is too complex for prompting alone. Hierarchy: try prompting first → if fails, try RAG for knowledge gaps → if fails, fine-tune. Often combine: fine-tune for style + RAG for knowledge.

blocks.text: LoRA/QLoRA knowledge shows hands-on LLM experience. Know: low-rank decomposition intuition, that only A and B are trained, that base weights are frozen, QLoRA for memory efficiency. The RAG vs fine-tuning comparison is asked in nearly every LLM engineering interview — have a crisp one-paragraph answer.

AI agents are LLM-powered systems that can take multi-step actions to complete complex goals, using tools and memory. An agent perceives its environment (text, tool outputs), reasons (LLM), and acts (call tools, write files, browse web). Core components: LLM (the reasoning engine), Tools (functions the agent can call), Memory (conversation history + persistent storage), Planning (decompose tasks into steps). Function calling (tool use): the LLM generates a structured call specifying which function to call and what arguments to pass. The application executes the function and returns the result to the LLM. Allows LLMs to: search the web, query databases, call APIs, execute code, read/write files. ReAct pattern (Reason + Act): the LLM alternates between reasoning ('I need to find X') and acting (call search tool) and observing (result of search). Frameworks: LangChain, LlamaIndex, AutoGen, CrewAI. MCP (Model Context Protocol): Anthropic's open standard for connecting LLMs to external tools and data sources — a universal plug-and-play interface.

Agent = LLM + tools + memory + planning loop. The loop: Observe → Think → Act → Observe (repeat). Tools extend the LLM beyond its training knowledge (real-time data, computation, external systems). Function calling = the LLM's interface to tools (structured JSON output → your code executes → result fed back). Multi-agent systems: multiple specialised agents collaborate (one plans, one codes, one verifies).

qas.q: What is function calling in LLMs?
qas.a: Function calling allows LLMs to output structured JSON describing a function call instead of (or in addition to) free text. The developer defines functions with name, description, and parameter schema. When the LLM decides it needs to use a function, it outputs: {name: 'get_weather', arguments: {city: 'London'}}. The application executes this and returns the result to the LLM in the next message. This enables tool use without fine-tuning.

qas.q: What are the main challenges of AI agents?
qas.a: Reliability: LLMs make mistakes in long chains of reasoning — errors compound. Latency: multiple LLM calls + tool calls = slow. Cost: each step costs tokens. Hallucinated tool calls: LLM invents function names or wrong arguments. Infinite loops: agent gets stuck. Mitigation: human-in-the-loop checkpoints, timeouts, max iterations, structured output validation, lower temperature for planning steps.

blocks.text: AI agents are the hottest topic in 2025 AI engineering. Know: ReAct pattern, function calling mechanism, MCP protocol, agent frameworks (LangChain, CrewAI). System design: 'Design a coding agent that can write and run Python code' — answer with: LLM + code execution tool + file read/write tool + memory + error feedback loop.

Hallucinations are cases where LLMs generate confident-sounding but factually incorrect information. They occur because LLMs are trained to produce fluent, probable text — not to be factually accurate. The model has no internal 'fact-checker'. Types: Factual hallucination (inventing non-existent facts, fake citations, wrong dates). Instructional hallucination (ignoring instructions). Contextual hallucination (contradicting information in the provided context). Causes: training objective is next-token prediction, not truth. Training data contains errors and contradictions. The model fills knowledge gaps with plausible-sounding completions. Mitigations: RAG (ground responses in retrieved sources), citation enforcement (require the model to cite), temperature=0 (more deterministic), self-consistency (multiple samples, majority vote), LLM-as-judge verification, structured output validation, human review for high-stakes decisions. Quantisation refers to reducing model weights from 32-bit floats to 8-bit or 4-bit integers — reduces memory and speeds inference with minimal quality loss.

Hallucination root cause: LLMs optimise for fluency, not truth. The model does not know what it does not know — it generates confidently regardless. RAG is the strongest mitigation for factual hallucinations. For safety-critical applications, always verify LLM outputs with deterministic checks (regex, database lookup, human review).

qas.q: Why do LLMs hallucinate and how do you reduce hallucinations?
qas.a: LLMs predict the most probable next token given context — probability ≠ truth. When the model lacks information, it generates plausible completions that may be false. Reductions: RAG (give the model correct information to reason from), prompt instruction ('only answer from the provided context, say I don't know if unsure'), temperature=0 for factual queries, self-consistency voting, retrieval from authoritative sources, and LLM-as-judge for automated quality checks.

qas.q: What is LLM-as-judge?
qas.a: Using a separate (often stronger) LLM to evaluate the quality, factual accuracy, or helpfulness of another LLM's output. The judge LLM is given the original prompt and the model's response and is asked to score or critique it. Used in evaluation pipelines where human annotation is too slow/expensive. Risks: judge LLM has its own biases (GPT-4 may favour GPT-4-style answers). Mitigation: multiple diverse judges, calibration against human labels.

blocks.text: Hallucination is asked in any LLM product discussion. The complete answer: explain the root cause (fluency ≠ accuracy), the main mitigation (RAG), and that for safety-critical applications (medical, legal) LLM outputs must be verified. Mentioning quantisation as a separate concept (reducing weight precision for deployment efficiency) shows deployment awareness.

Regression metrics measure the distance between predicted and actual continuous values. MAE (Mean Absolute Error) = (1/n)Σ|yᵢ−ŷᵢ|. Interpretable (same units as target), robust to outliers. MSE (Mean Squared Error) = (1/n)Σ(yᵢ−ŷᵢ)². Penalises large errors quadratically, sensitive to outliers, differentiable (gradient descent friendly). RMSE (Root MSE) = √MSE. Same units as target, more interpretable than MSE. R² (Coefficient of Determination): 1 − (SS_res/SS_tot). Proportion of variance explained. 1 = perfect, 0 = no better than predicting the mean, negative = worse than mean. Adjusted R²: penalises adding useless features. MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error) = (1/n)Σ|yᵢ−ŷᵢ|/|yᵢ|. Percentage error, scale-independent but undefined when yᵢ=0. Huber Loss: MSE for small errors, MAE for large errors — robust to outliers while remaining differentiable.

MAE: robust, interpretable. MSE: penalises outliers, used for gradient descent. RMSE: same units as MSE. R²: proportion explained. Choose MAE when outliers are real signal. Choose RMSE when large errors are especially bad. Choose R² for relative comparison across datasets.

qas.q: When would you use MAE vs RMSE?
qas.a: RMSE: when large errors are disproportionately bad (e.g., medical dosage — a 10x error is much worse than 10 1x errors). RMSE penalises outliers heavily. MAE: when all errors are equally bad regardless of magnitude (e.g., predicting delivery time — a 1-hour error is twice as bad as 30 minutes, not 4× as bad). MAE is also more interpretable — it's the average error in the target's units.

blocks.text: Regression metrics are expected knowledge. The nuance interviewers probe: when to use MAE vs MSE/RMSE (based on outlier treatment and error penalty function). Mention that MSE is used as a loss function during training because it's differentiable; MAE's gradient is constant (±1) which can cause instability.

Cross-validation estimates generalisation error by splitting data into multiple train/test folds and averaging performance. K-Fold CV: split data into K equal folds, train K times with each fold as the test set, average metrics. K=5 or K=10 is standard. Pros: uses all data for both training and testing. Cons: K × training cost. Stratified K-Fold: maintains class distribution in each fold — essential for imbalanced classification. Leave-One-Out CV (LOOCV): K=n, each sample is its own test set. Highest variance estimate, extremely slow. Group K-Fold: ensures all samples from the same group (same patient, same user) are in the same fold — prevents leakage when group structure exists. Time-Series CV (Walk-Forward): always train on past, test on future. Train[1:t], Test[t+1], Train[1:t+1], Test[t+2]... Never shuffle. Nested CV: outer CV for performance estimation, inner CV for hyperparameter tuning — gives unbiased estimate of model selection performance.

K=5 or K=10 for standard CV. Stratified for class imbalance. Time-series CV never shuffles. Group CV when entities span multiple rows. Nested CV for unbiased hyperparameter-tuned model evaluation. Always use CV for hyperparameter tuning — never tune on the test set.

qas.q: What is the difference between K-Fold and Stratified K-Fold?
qas.a: K-Fold splits randomly — in imbalanced datasets (e.g., 95% negative, 5% positive), some folds may have 0% positive examples, making evaluation unreliable. Stratified K-Fold ensures each fold has the same class ratio as the full dataset. Essential for binary/multi-class classification with imbalance (>5:1 ratio). sklearn's StratifiedKFold handles this automatically.

qas.q: How do you perform cross-validation correctly for time-series data?
qas.a: Never shuffle time-series data — future data cannot be used to predict the past. Use time-series split (walk-forward CV): each fold trains on all data up to time t and tests on the next window [t, t+h]. In sklearn: TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5). The training set grows with each fold (expanding window) or slides (rolling window). This ensures the model never sees future data during training.

blocks.text: Cross-validation questions reveal whether you understand the purpose (unbiased generalisation estimate) vs just the mechanics. The time-series trap is classic: using random CV on time-series data leaks future information into training — this is a disqualifying mistake in finance/forecasting interviews.

Hyperparameter tuning finds the hyperparameter values that optimise model performance on the validation set. Grid Search: defines a grid of all hyperparameter combinations and evaluates every one via cross-validation. Exhaustive but exponentially slow with many hyperparameters. Random Search: samples random combinations from defined distributions. Empirically, often finds equally good results in 10-20% of the Grid Search cost (most hyperparameters have little effect — Random Search finds the few that matter faster). Bayesian Optimisation: builds a probabilistic surrogate model of the objective function, uses it to decide where to evaluate next (balancing exploration vs exploitation). More sample-efficient than random search for expensive evaluations. Libraries: Optuna (Bayesian, parallel), Hyperopt, Ray Tune. Halving search: trains with small compute, progressively discards poor configurations, invests more in promising ones — fast for many hyperparameters. HyperBand, Successive Halving are formal algorithms.

Practical hyperparameter tuning strategy: Start with Random Search (50-100 evaluations) to find good regions. Then Bayesian Optimisation to refine. Always use early stopping within each training run to save cost. For XGBoost/LGBM: tune n_estimators with early stopping first, then learning_rate, max_depth, subsample, colsample_bytree.

qas.q: Why does random search outperform grid search in practice?
qas.a: In high-dimensional hyperparameter spaces, most hyperparameters have little effect on performance — only 1-2 matter significantly (learning rate, number of trees). Grid search wastes evaluations by exhaustively covering useless hyperparameters. Random search samples all dimensions independently — for each 'important' hyperparameter, it gets many distinct values regardless of the unimportant ones. With the same budget, random search explores the important dimensions more efficiently.

blocks.text: Hyperparameter tuning questions often follow model selection questions. Know: Grid Search (exhaustive, slow), Random Search (practical, faster), Bayesian Optimisation (sample-efficient, complex). For interviews: mention that for DL you use learning rate range test + warm-up, not grid search, because retraining is too expensive.

The right metric depends on the problem type, class distribution, and business cost of different errors. Regression: RMSE when large errors are especially costly, MAE for equal error treatment, MAPE for percentage comparison across scales. Binary classification (balanced): accuracy is acceptable. Binary classification (imbalanced): F1 score, ROC-AUC, or PR-AUC. Precision-critical (false positives are costly): precision or positive predictive value. Recall-critical (false negatives are costly): recall or sensitivity. Example: Cancer detection → maximise recall (never miss cancer). Spam filter → maximise precision (never block real emails). Fraud detection → balance with F1 or use PR-AUC (very imbalanced). Multi-class: macro-averaged F1 (equal weight per class), weighted F1 (weight by class frequency). Ranking: NDCG (Normalised Discounted Cumulative Gain), MAP (Mean Average Precision). NLP generation: BLEU (n-gram overlap for translation), ROUGE (recall-oriented for summarisation), BERTScore (semantic similarity).

The metric question template: (1) Balanced or imbalanced? If imbalanced → skip accuracy. (2) What's more costly: false positive or false negative? → Precision or Recall. (3) Need to balance both? → F1. (4) Need threshold-independent measure? → ROC-AUC or PR-AUC. (5) Very imbalanced? → PR-AUC (ROC-AUC is misleadingly optimistic).

qas.q: You are building a fraud detection system. What metric do you choose?
qas.a: Fraud is rare (1-5% positive rate — imbalanced). Accuracy is useless. I would use PR-AUC as the primary metric: it focuses entirely on the positive class (fraud) and is not inflated by the abundant true negatives. During development, I also track precision-recall tradeoff to set the operating threshold based on business cost (cost of missed fraud vs cost of investigating false alarms). F1 at the chosen threshold gives the final single-number summary.

qas.q: What is NDCG and when is it used?
qas.a: NDCG (Normalised Discounted Cumulative Gain) evaluates ranked result lists. It rewards relevant items placed higher in the ranking, with a logarithmic discount for lower positions (position 2 is less important than position 1). Normalised by the ideal ranking. Used in: search engines, recommendation systems, any task where the order of results matters. Kaggle competitions on recommender systems frequently use NDCG@10 (NDCG considering only top-10 results).

blocks.text: Metric selection is tested via scenario questions: 'What metric for medical diagnosis? For recommendation? For fraud?' The wrong answer is always 'accuracy'. The right answer always starts with the class distribution and cost of each error type. This framework — not memorised answers — is what interviewers want to see.

Model deployment makes a trained model available for production use. Deployment patterns: REST API (most common) — wrap model in a FastAPI/Flask service, expose /predict endpoint, containerise with Docker. Batch inference — run predictions on a batch of data on a schedule (nightly), no latency requirement. Real-time/online inference — synchronous prediction for each request, low-latency requirement (<100ms). Streaming inference — process data as it arrives (Kafka → model → output stream). Serving infrastructure: Docker containers for reproducibility. Kubernetes for scaling. Cloud ML serving (AWS SageMaker, GCP Vertex AI, Azure ML). Serverless (AWS Lambda) for infrequent/spiky traffic. Model serialisation: sklearn → joblib/pickle, PyTorch → torch.save/TorchScript, TensorFlow → SavedModel, ONNX (Open Neural Network Exchange) — framework-agnostic format for portability. Canary deployment: route 5% of traffic to the new model, monitor, gradually increase — safe rollout. A/B testing: compare two model versions on live traffic.

Deployment checklist: (1) Serialise model (ONNX for portability). (2) Wrap in REST API (FastAPI). (3) Containerise (Docker). (4) Define health check + /metrics endpoint. (5) Set up logging for input/output/latency. (6) Deploy behind a load balancer. (7) Canary or A/B test before full rollout. (8) Set up monitoring (step after deployment).

qas.q: What is the difference between online and batch inference?
qas.a: Online inference: real-time, synchronous — user sends a request and waits for the prediction (<100ms latency). Used for: recommendation at request time, fraud scoring before transaction approval. Batch inference: offline, asynchronous — run predictions on a large dataset overnight. Used for: email campaign targeting, credit score updates, large-scale ETL pipelines. Batch is simpler and cheaper; online requires low-latency infrastructure.

qas.q: Why use ONNX for model deployment?
qas.a: ONNX (Open Neural Network Exchange) is a standard format for ML models. Trained in PyTorch → exported to ONNX → served with ONNX Runtime (faster inference than PyTorch) or deployed to edge devices. Benefits: framework independence (train in framework A, deploy in B), optimised inference (ONNX Runtime applies graph optimisations), cross-platform (CPU, GPU, mobile, edge). Essential when training and serving environments differ.

blocks.text: Model deployment questions show production ML awareness. The complete answer includes: containerisation, API serving, serialisation format, traffic routing strategy (canary/A/B), and monitoring. Mentioning ONNX Runtime for optimised inference shows practical deployment knowledge.

An ML pipeline automates the end-to-end flow from raw data to deployed predictions. Components: Data Ingestion → Feature Engineering → Training → Evaluation → Deployment → Monitoring. Pipeline orchestration tools: Apache Airflow (workflow DAGs), Kubeflow Pipelines (Kubernetes-native ML), Metaflow, Prefect, ZenML. Feature Store: a centralised repository that stores, versions, and serves computed features. Solves the training-serving skew problem: features computed offline for training should be identical to features computed online for serving. Offline store (batch features): Hive, BigQuery, S3. Online store (low-latency serving): Redis, DynamoDB. Feature stores: Feast, Tecton, Hopsworks. Benefits of feature stores: feature reuse across teams, consistent training/serving features, feature versioning, point-in-time correct feature retrieval (prevents temporal data leakage). sklearn Pipeline: within a single training job, chains preprocessing + model into one object — prevents data leakage, enables easy deployment.

Training-serving skew = features computed differently at training time vs serving time → model sees different data in production. Feature stores solve this by being the single source of truth for features in both contexts. The difference between a data pipeline (ETL) and an ML pipeline: ML pipeline includes model training, evaluation, and deployment as automated steps.

qas.q: What is training-serving skew and how do you prevent it?
qas.a: Training-serving skew occurs when features are computed differently during training (in a batch notebook) vs serving (in a REST API). Example: training uses 7-day rolling average computed in pandas; serving uses a bug-fixed version that computes a different window. The model was trained on data it never sees in production. Prevention: use a feature store to centralise feature computation. Test features end-to-end by comparing training features vs serving features on the same input.

blocks.text: ML pipelines and feature stores are asked in ML engineering roles at larger companies. Know: pipeline = automated ETL + train + deploy workflow, feature store = centralised feature versioning solving training-serving skew. System design: 'Design an ML pipeline for real-time fraud detection' should include: streaming feature computation, feature store, model serving, monitoring.

Model monitoring tracks production model performance over time to detect degradation. Why models degrade: the world changes — user behaviour evolves, data distributions shift, rare events become common. Types of drift: Data drift (covariate shift) — input feature distribution P(X) changes. Model predicts correctly but for different populations than it was trained on. Concept drift — the relationship between inputs and target P(Y|X) changes. The fundamental signal the model learned no longer holds. Label drift — output distribution P(Y) changes. What to monitor: Prediction distribution — are prediction scores drifting? (no labels needed). Data distribution — are input feature statistics (mean, std, histograms) stable? Performance metrics — accuracy/AUC against fresh labels (requires labels, lagged). Business metrics — are downstream KPIs healthy? Detection methods: Statistical tests (KS test, PSI — Population Stability Index, JS divergence) on feature distributions. Monitoring frequency: every day for high-stakes models. Retraining trigger: PSI > 0.2 or performance drop > 5%.

Monitoring hierarchy: Prediction distribution (immediate, no labels) → Data distribution (daily, statistical tests) → Performance metrics (weekly/monthly, requires labels). PSI (Population Stability Index) > 0.25 = significant drift, retrain urgently. 0.1-0.25 = moderate, investigate. <0.1 = stable. Drift ≠ performance drop — sometimes distribution changes but performance is stable.

qas.q: What is the difference between data drift and concept drift?
qas.a: Data drift (covariate shift): input features X change distribution but the relationship P(Y|X) stays the same. Example: user age distribution shifts (more young users), but given same age, click rate is the same. Model may become less accurate because it hasn't seen the new distribution. Concept drift: P(Y|X) itself changes. Example: users' click behaviour changes — same demographics, different preferences. This is harder to detect (requires fresh labels) and more damaging — the entire model signal may be invalid.

qas.q: How do you monitor a model when labels arrive with a delay?
qas.a: Many production systems have delayed labels (e.g., fraud labels come days after the transaction, customer churn is known months later). In the absence of labels, monitor: prediction score distribution (are confidence scores drifting?), input feature distributions (PSI), and proxy metrics (downstream KPIs, user engagement). When delayed labels arrive, compute true metrics and compare with a sliding window. Set up retrospective evaluation pipelines that score model performance as labels materialise.

blocks.text: Model monitoring separates ML engineers who deploy and forget from those who productionise responsibly. Know: data drift vs concept drift, PSI for distribution monitoring, that most monitoring can be done without labels (prediction distribution, feature statistics). Mentioning a concrete alerting threshold (PSI > 0.25, performance drop > 5%) shows practical deployment experience.

chapters.title: Versioning, MLflow, and CI/CD for ML

blocks.text: ML versioning covers code, data, models, and experiments — all must be versioned to reproduce results. Code versioning: Git (standard). Data versioning: DVC (Data Version Control) — tracks large data files in git-like fashion, stores data in S3/GCS/local, links dataset versions to code commits. Model versioning: MLflow Model Registry — register model versions, promote through stages (Staging → Production → Archived). MLflow Tracking: log metrics, parameters, and artefacts for each training run (experiment tracking). Other tools: Weights & Biases (W&B) for rich experiment tracking with visualisation. CI/CD for ML: Continuous Integration — automatically retrain and evaluate models on new data or code changes. Test: unit tests for features, integration tests for pipeline, model evaluation tests (performance above threshold). Continuous Delivery — automatically deploy if tests pass. ML-specific checks: data quality tests (Great Expectations), model performance regression tests, bias/fairness tests. Docker + Kubernetes + GitHub Actions = standard CI/CD stack for ML.

ML reproducibility requires versioning ALL four: code (Git), data (DVC), experiments (MLflow/W&B), models (MLflow Registry). The CD step for ML is more nuanced than software: you gate on model performance metrics in addition to passing tests. A model that passes tests but has worse AUC should not be deployed.

qas.q: What is MLflow and what problems does it solve?
qas.a: MLflow is an open-source platform for the ML lifecycle. It solves: (1) Experiment tracking — log hyperparameters, metrics, artefacts for each run, compare runs. (2) Model packaging — package model with dependencies in a portable format (MLmodel). (3) Model Registry — version models, manage lifecycle stages (Staging/Production). (4) Deployment — serve models as REST APIs. Without MLflow, experiments are tracked in spreadsheets and models are unnamed pickle files — reproduction is impossible.

qas.q: What are the differences between software CI/CD and ML CI/CD?
qas.a: Software CI/CD: test code correctness, build, deploy. ML CI/CD adds: data validation (schema tests, distribution tests), model training (triggered by data changes, not just code changes), model evaluation (performance threshold tests — if new model AUC < old model AUC, block deployment), model monitoring setup, and data version pinning. ML CI/CD is slower (training takes hours), harder to test (evaluation requires data), and has more failure modes (model regression without code changes).

blocks.text: MLflow/DVC knowledge is expected for ML engineering roles. For data science roles, at minimum know experiment tracking (MLflow Tracking). CI/CD for ML is asked in senior/lead roles. The key insight: in ML, a 'regression' can be a model performance drop without any code change (data drift caused it) — your CI/CD must catch this.
